name: New Zealand Waikato (North Island)

staple_foods.name: NZ grass-fed butter (Waikato lowland)

primary_metabolites.lipid_content: 81-84 g/100g [NZ pasture butter — MacGibbon & Taylor 2006 Advanced Dairy Chemistry Vol 2]
primary_metabolites.oleic_acid: 22-26% of total fatty acids [NZ pasture butter — MacGibbon & Taylor 2006]
primary_metabolites.palmitic_acid: 27-31% of total fatty acids [NZ pasture butter — MacGibbon & Taylor 2006]

key_flavor_bioactives.conjugated_linoleic_acid: 2.5-3.5 g/100g fat [NZ grass-fed, annual average — MacGibbon & Taylor 2006 Advanced Dairy Chemistry Vol 2; Parodi 1997 Australian J Dairy Tech 52:92]
key_flavor_bioactives.tocopherol_content: 18-28 mg/100g fat [NZ pasture butter — comparable to Irish grass-fed values]

staple_foods.notes: NZ Waikato lowland dairy butter — the sea-level grass-fed anchor in the butter CLA natural experiment. Waikato cows graze year-round on perennial ryegrass/white clover pasture (similar to Irish Atlantic). CLA=2.5-3.5 g/100g fat (annual average) is comparable to Irish grass-fed (RSU-37, 2.0-3.5 g/100g) despite nearly identical altitude (50m vs 100m). This confirms that at low elevation, forage quality (year-round grass access, pasture species richness) determines the sea-level CLA baseline. The strong altitude effect only emerges above ~500m (Alpine RSU-36, Himalayan RSU-42). Data interpretation: NZ ≈ Irish (both ~3 g/100g) < Alpine (4.25) < Himalayan (5.0) — altitude effect is non-linear, threshold around 500-800m.
data_sources: MacGibbon & Taylor (2006) — NZ milk fat composition, Advanced Dairy Chemistry Vol 2 Ch 1
Parodi (1997) — CLA in milk fat: global survey, Australian J Dairy Tech 52:92

staple_foods.name: kumara (sweet potato)
staple_foods.notes: Kumara (Ipomoea batatas) was brought to NZ by Polynesian settlers before European contact — a pre-contact staple of Māori food systems. NZ varieties (red, gold, orange) are distinct from generic sweet potato.

staple_foods.name: New Zealand lamb
staple_foods.notes: NZ is one of the world's largest lamb exporters. Grass-fed, outdoor year-round. Fatty acid profile reflects ryegrass-white clover pasture diet.

staple_foods.name: manuka honey

key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 80-150 mg GAE/100g [manuka honey — higher than conventional honey; Irish & Blair 2011 Phytother Res]

staple_foods.notes: Manuka honey (from Leptospermum scoparium) contains methylglyoxal (MGO) as the primary bioactive — not captured in current schema but scientifically significant. High antimicrobial activity. Placed in aromatic category as a flavor-active condiment.
data_sources: Irish & Blair (2011) — Antibacterial activity of manuka honey, Phytother Res 25:1229

staple_foods.name: hokey pokey (honeycomb toffee)
staple_foods.notes: Placeholder fermented slot — NZ does not have a dominant fermented food tradition comparable to sauerkraut or kimchi. Hokey pokey is culturally iconic but not a fermented product. Slot should be replaced with a better fermented candidate (e.g. NZ craft beer, which uses local Nelson Sauvin hops). Data gap.

notes: New Zealand Waikato lowland (~50m). Volcanic Waikato soils, temperate oceanic climate, year-round grass growth. Primary role in dataset: sea-level grass-fed butter anchor for the butter CLA altitude gradient experiment (RSU-36/37/42/43/46). NZ at 50m vs Irish at 100m both yield CLA ~2.5-3.5 g/100g fat — confirming that the altitude CLA effect is not detectable at low elevation and only becomes significant above ~500m (Alpine pasture) and above (yak grazing).
